$$\int {{{{x^2}} \over {x(1 + {x^2})}}dx = \int {{x \over {1 + {x^2}}}dx = {1 \over 2}\int {{{2x} \over {1 + {x^2}}}dx} } } $$

$$ = {1 \over 2}\log \left| {1 + {x^2}} \right| + k$$,

k is constant of integration.
$$\int {{{dx} \over {x(x + 1)}} = \int {{1 \over x}dx - \int {{{dx} \over {x + 1}} = \log \left( {{x \over {x + 1}}} \right) + c} } } $$

$$\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } \left[ {{n \over {{n^2} + {1^2}}} + {n \over {{n^2} + {2^2}}} + ..... + {n \over {{n^2} + {n^2}}}} \right]$$

$$ = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } {1 \over n}\left[ {{1 \over {1 + {{\left( {{1 \over n}} \right)}^2}}} + {1 \over {1 + {{\left( {{2 \over n}} \right)}^2}}} + .... + {1 \over {1 + {{\left( {{n \over n}} \right)}^2}}}} \right]$$

$$ = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } {1 \over n}\sum\limits_{r = 1}^n {{1 \over {1 + {{\left( {{r \over n}} \right)}^2}}} = \int\limits_0^1 {{1 \over {1 + {x^2}}}dx} } $$

$$ = [{\tan ^{ - 1}}x]_0^1 = {\tan ^{ - 1}}1 - {\tan ^{ - 1}}0 = {\pi \over 4}$$
$$\int\limits_{ - 1}^1 {{{|x + 2|} \over {x + 2}}dx = \int\limits_{ - 1}^0 {dx + \int\limits_0^1 {dx = 2} } } $$

$$\int {{{dx} \over {\sin x + \sqrt 3 \cos x}} = \int {{{{1 \over 2}dx} \over {{1 \over 2}\sin x + {{\sqrt 3 } \over 2}\cos x}}} } $$

$$ = {1 \over 2}\int {{{dx} \over {\cos x\cos {\pi \over 6} + \sin x\,.\,\sin {\pi \over 6}}}} $$

$$ = {1 \over 2}\int {{{dx} \over {\cos \left( {x - {\pi \over 6}} \right)}} = {1 \over 2}\int {\sec \left( {x - {\pi \over 6}} \right)dx} } $$

$$ = {1 \over 2}\log \left| {\tan \left( {{\pi \over 4} + {{x - {\pi \over 6}} \over 2}} \right)} \right| + c$$

$$ = {1 \over 2}\log \left| {\tan \left( {{\pi \over 4} + {x \over 2} - {\pi \over {12}}} \right)} \right| + c = {1 \over 2}\log \left| {\tan \left( {{\pi \over 6} + {x \over 2}} \right)} \right| + c$$

$${{dx} \over x} = 2dz$$]

$$ = {2 \over 3}\,.\,{{{z^2}} \over 2} + C = {1 \over 3}{\left( {\log \sqrt x } \right)^2} + C$$

content: If $$I = \int {{{{x^2}dx} \over {{{(x\sin x + \cos x)}^2}}} = f(x) + \tan x + c} $$, then $$f(x)$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Given, } I=\int \frac{x^2 d x}{(x \sin x+\cos x)^2} \\ & =\int \frac{x}{(x \sin x+\cos x)^2} \times \frac{x \cos x}{\cos x} d x \\ & \quad \frac{d}{d x}(x \sin x+\cos x) \\ & =x \cos x+\sin x-\sin x \\ & =x \cos x \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad I= & \frac{x}{\cos x} \int \frac{x \cos x}{(x \sin x+\cos x)^2} d x \\ & \quad-\int\left(\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{x}{\cos x}\right) \int \frac{x \cos d x}{(x \sin x+\cos x)^2}\right) d x \quad \text{[Integrating by part]} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{x}{\cos x}\left(\frac{-1}{x \sin x+\cos x}\right) \\ & -\int \frac{(\cos x+x \sin x)}{\cos ^2 x}\left(\frac{-1}{x \sin x+\cos x}\right) d x \\ & \Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{-x}{\cos x(x \sin x+\cos x)}+\int \sec ^2 x \\ & \Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{-x}{\cos x(x \sin x+\cos x)}+\tan x+C \\ & \therefore f(x)=\frac{-x}{\cos x(x \sin x+\cos x)} \\ & \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\int {{{dx} \over {(x + 1)(x - 2)(x - 3)}} = {1 \over k}{{\log }_e}\left\{ {{{|x - 3{|^3}|x + 1|} \over {{{(x - 2)}^4}}}} \right\} + c} $$, then the value of k is
explanation:
$$\int \frac{d x}{(x+1)(x-2)(x-3)}$$

Let $$\frac{1}{(x+1)(x-2)(x-3)} \equiv \frac{A}{x+1}+\frac{B}{x-2}+\frac{C}{x-3}$$

$$\Rightarrow 1 \equiv A(x-2)(x-3)+B(x+1) (x-3) +C(x+1)(x-2)$$

$$1 \equiv A\left(x^2-5 x+6\right)+B\left(x^2-2 x-3\right) +C\left(x^2-x-4\right)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 1 \equiv x^2(A+B+C)+x(-5 A-2 B-C) \\ &+(6 A-3 B-2 C) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{gathered} \Rightarrow \quad A+B+C=0,-5 A-2 B-C=0, \\ 6 A-3 B-2 C=1 \end{gathered}$$

$$\Rightarrow A=\frac{1}{12}, B=\frac{-1}{3}, C=\frac{1}{4}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \int \frac{d x}{(x+1)(x-2)(x-3)}= & \frac{1}{12} \int \frac{d x}{x+1} -\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{d x}{x-2}+\frac{1}{4} \int \frac{d x}{x-3} \end{aligned}$$

$$=\frac{1}{12} \log _e|x+1|-\frac{1}{3} \log _e|x-2| +\frac{1}{4} \log _e|x-3|+C$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{12} \log _e\left(\frac{|x+1||x-3|^3}{|x-2|^4}\right)+C \\ \therefore R & =12 \end{aligned}$$

options.content: $${x \over 2}\{ \cos (\ln x) + \sin (\ln x)\}  + c$$ (c denotes constant of integration)
$${x^2}\{ \cos (\ln x) - \sin (\ln x)\}  + c$$ (c denotes constant of integration)
$${x^2}\sin (\ln x) + c$$ (c denotes constant of integration)
$$x\cos (\ln x) + c$$ (c denotes constant of integration)

explanation:
$$I = \int {\cos (\ln x)dx} $$

Let, $$\ln x = t$$

$$ \Rightarrow x = {e^t}$$

$$ \Rightarrow dx = {e^t}dt$$

$$\therefore$$ $$I = \int {\cos (t)\,.\,{e^t}dt} $$

Apply integration by parts theorem,

$$ = \cos t\,.\,\int {{e^t}dt - \int {\left( {{d \over {dt}}(\cos t)\,.\,\int {{e^t}dt} } \right)dt} } $$

$$ = \cos t\,.\,{e^t} + \int {\sin t\,.\,{e^t}dt} $$

$$I = \cos t\,.\,{e^t} + \sin t\,.\,{e^t} - \int {(\cos t\,.\,{e^t})\,dt} $$

$$ \Rightarrow I = \cos t\,.\,{e^t} + \sin t\,.\,{e^t} - I$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2I = \cos t\,.\,{e^t} + \sin t\,.\,{e^t} + C$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2I = {e^t}(\cos t + \sin t) + C$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2I = x\left( {\cos (\ln x) + \sin (\ln x)} \right) + C$$

$$ \Rightarrow I = {x \over 2}\left[ {\cos (\ln x) + \sin (\ln x)} \right] + C$$

Let $$\int {{{{x^{{1 \over 2}}}} \over {\sqrt {1 - {x^3}} }}dx = {2 \over 3}g(f(x)) + c} $$ ; then

(c denotes constant of integration)
If $$\int {{{\sin 2x} \over {{{(a + b\cos x)}^2}}}dx}  = \alpha \left[ {{{\log }_e}\left| {a + b\cos x} \right| + {a \over {a + b\cos x}}} \right] + c$$, then $$\alpha$$ is equal to
explanation:
Let $$I = \int {{{\sin 2x} \over {{{(a + b\cos x)}^2}}}dx} $$

$$ \Rightarrow I = \int {{{2\sin x\cos x} \over {{{(a + b\cos x)}^2}}}dx} $$

Put $$a + b\cos x = t$$

$$ \Rightarrow \cos x = {{t - a} \over b} \Rightarrow - b\sin xdx = dt$$

$$I = - {2 \over b}\int {\left( {{{t - a} \over b}} \right){1 \over {{t^2}}}dt} $$

$$ = - {2 \over {{b^2}}}\int {{{t - a} \over {{t^2}}}dt} $$

$$ = - {2 \over {{b^2}}}\left[ {\int {{1 \over t}dt - \int {{a \over {{t^2}}}dt} } } \right]$$

$$ = - {2 \over {{b^2}}}\int {\left[ {\log (t) - a{{{t^{ - 2 + 1}}} \over { - 2 + 1}}} \right] + C} $$

$$ = - {2 \over {{b^2}}}\left[ {\log \left| t \right| + {a \over t}} \right] + C$$

$$\therefore$$ $$I = - {2 \over {{b^2}}}\left[ {\log \left| {a + b\cos x} \right| + {a \over {a + b\cos x}}} \right] + C$$

$$ \Rightarrow \alpha = - {2 \over {{b^2}}}$$

$$I = \int {{{f(x)\phi '(x) + \phi (x)\,f'(x)} \over {(f(x)\phi (x) + 1)\sqrt {f(x)\phi (x) - 1} }}dx} $$

Put f(x)$$\phi $$(x) $$ - $$ 1 = t2

$$ \Rightarrow $$ (f(x)$$\phi $$'(x) + $$\phi $$(x) f'(x)) dx = 2t dt

$$ \therefore $$ $$I = \int {{{2tdt} \over {({t^2} + 2)t}}} $$

$$ \Rightarrow I = 2\int {{{dt} \over {{t^2} + 2}}} $$

= $${2 \over {\sqrt 2 }}{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {{t \over {\sqrt 2 }}} \right) + c$$

$$ \Rightarrow I = \sqrt 2 {\tan ^{ - 1}}\sqrt {{{f(x)\phi (x) - 1} \over 2}} + c$$

content: If $$\int {\cos x\log \left( {\tan {x \over 2}} \right)} dx$$ = $$\sin x\log \left( {\tan {x \over 2}} \right)$$ + f(x), then f(x) is equal to (assuming c is a arbitrary real constant).
explanation:
Let $$I = \int {\cos x\log \left( {\tan {x \over 2}} \right)} dx$$

$$ = \log \left( {\tan {x \over 2}} \right).\,\sin x - \int {\sin x\,.\,{1 \over {\tan {x \over 2}}}\,.\,{{\sec }^2}{x \over 2}} \,.\,{1 \over 2}dx$$

$$ = \sin x\log \left( {\tan {x \over 2}} \right) - \int {\sin x{1 \over {2\sin {x \over 2}\cos {x \over 2}}}} dx$$

$$ = \sin x.\log \left( {\tan {x \over 2}} \right) - \int {{{\sin x} \over {\sin x}}dx} $$

$$ = \sin x.\log \left( {\tan {x \over 2}} \right) - \int {1dx} $$

$$ = \sin x\log \left( {\tan {x \over 2}} \right) - x + c$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$f(x) = c - x$$

content: y = $$\int {\cos \left\{ {2{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\sqrt {{{1 - x} \over {1 + x}}} } \right\}} dx$$ is an equation of a family of
explanation:
Let $$I = \int {\cos \left\{ {2{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\sqrt {{{1 - x} \over {1 + x}}} } \right\}} dx$$

Put $$x = \cos 2\theta \Rightarrow dx = - 2\sin 2\theta d\theta $$

$$ \therefore $$ $$I = \int {\cos \left\{ {2{{\tan }^1}\sqrt {{{1 - \cos 2\theta } \over {1 + \cos 2\theta }}} } \right\}} ( - 2\sin 2\theta )d\theta $$

$$ = - 2\int {\cos \left\{ {2{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\sqrt {{{2{{\sin }^2}\theta } \over {2{{\cos }^2}\theta }}} } \right\}} \sin 2\theta d\theta $$

$$ = - 2\int {\cos (2{{\tan }^{ - 1}}(\tan \theta )\} \sin 2\theta d\theta } $$

$$ = - 2\int {\cos 2\theta \sin 2\theta d\theta } $$

$$ = \int {\cos 2\theta d(\cos 2\theta )} $$

$$ = {{{{\cos }^2}2\theta } \over 2} + C$$

$$ = {{{x^2}} \over 2} + C$$

Which is an equation of parabola.

content: If $$\int {{e^{\sin x}}} .\left[ {{{x{{\cos }^3}x - \sin x} \over {{{\cos }^2}x}}} \right]dx = {e^{\sin x}}f(x) + c$$, where c is constant of integration, then f(x) is equal to
explanation:
We have,

$$\int {{e^{\sin x}}} .\left( {{{x{{\cos }^3}x - \sin x} \over {{{\cos }^2}x}}} \right)dx = {e^{\sin x}}f(x) + c$$

$$ \Rightarrow \int {{e^{\sin x}}} (x\cos x - \sec x\tan x)dx = {e^{\sin x}}f(x) + c$$

$$ \Rightarrow \int {{e^{\sin x}}} (x\cos x - 1 + 1 - \sec x\tan x)dx = {e^{\sin x}}f(x) + c$$

$$ \Rightarrow \int {[{e^{\sin x}}\cos x(x - \sec x) + {e^{\sin x}}(1 - \sec x\tan x)]dx = {e^{\sin x}}f(x) + c} $$

$$ \Rightarrow \int {{d \over {dx}}\{ {e^{\sin x}}(x - \sec x)\} dx = {e^{\sin x}}f(x) + c} $$

$$ \Rightarrow {e^{\sin x}}(x - \sec x) = {e^{\sin x}}f(x) + c$$

$$ \Rightarrow f(x) = x - \sec x$$

content: If $$\int {f(x)} \sin x\cos xdx = {1 \over {2({b^2} - {a^2})}}\log (f(x)) + c$$, where c is the constant of integration, then f(x) is equal to
explanation:
We have,

$$\int {f(x)} \sin x\cos xdx = {1 \over {2({b^2} - {a^2})}}\log (f(x)) + c$$

$$ \Rightarrow f(x)\sin x\cos x = {1 \over {2({b^2} - {a^2})}}.{1 \over {f(x)}}.f'(x)$$

$$ \Rightarrow f(x)\sin 2x = {1 \over {{b^2} - {a^2}}}.{{f'(x)} \over {f(x)}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin 2x = {1 \over {{b^2} - {a^2}}}{{f'(x)} \over {{{(f(x))}^2}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \int {\sin 2xdx = {1 \over {{b^2} - {a^2}}}\int {{{f'(x)} \over {{{(f(x))}^2}}}} dx} $$

$$ \Rightarrow {{ - \cos 2x} \over 2} = {1 \over {{b^2} - {a^2}}}.\left( {{{ - 1} \over {f(x)}}} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{\cos 2x({b^2} - {a^2})} \over 2} = {1 \over {f(x)}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow f(x) = {2 \over {({b^2} - {a^2})\cos 2x}}$$

content: $$\int {\cos (\log x)dx} $$ = F(x) + C, where C is an arbitrary constant. Here, F(x) is equal to
explanation:
Let $$I = \int {\cos (\log x)dx} $$

Put log x = t

$$ \Rightarrow x = {e^t}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$dx = {e^t}dt$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$I = \int {{e^t}\cos t\,dt} $$

$$ = {{{e^t}} \over {{1^2} + {1^2}}}[\cos t + \sin t] + C$$

$$ \because $$ $$\left[ {\int {{e^{ax}}} \cos bx\,dx = {{{e^{ax}}} \over {{a^2} + {b^2}}}[a\cos bx + b\sin bx] + C} \right]$$

$$ \Rightarrow I = {{{e^t}} \over 2}[\cos t + \sin t] + C$$

$$ = {x \over 2}[\cos (\log x) + \sin (\log x] + C$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$f(x) = {x \over 2}[\cos (\log x) + \sin (\log x)]$$

Let $$I = \int {{{{x^2} - 1} \over {{x^4} + 3{x^2} + 1}}dx} $$

$$ = \int {{{1 - 1/{x^2}} \over {{x^2} + 3 + 1/{x^2}}}dx} $$

$$ = \int {{{1 - 1/{x^2}} \over {\left( {{x^2} + {1 \over {{x^2}}}} \right) + 3}}} dx$$

$$ = \int {{{1 - 1/{x^2}} \over {{{\left( {x + {1 \over x}} \right)}^2} - 2 + 3}}} dx$$

$$ = \int {{{1 - 1/{x^2}} \over {{{\left( {x + {1 \over x}} \right)}^2} + 1}}} dx$$

Let $$x + {1 \over x} = t$$

$$ \Rightarrow \left( {1 - {1 \over {{x^2}}}} \right)dx = dt$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$I = \int {{{dt} \over {{t^2} + 1}}} $$

$$ = {\tan ^{ - 1}}t + C$$

$${\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {x + {1 \over x}} \right) + C$$ $$ \because $$ $$\left[ {t = x + {1 \over x}} \right]$$

Let $$l = \int {{2^x}[f'(x) + f(x)\log 2]dx} $$

Consider, $$g(x) = {2^x}f(x)$$

$$ \Rightarrow g'(x) = {2^x}f'(x) + {2^x}f(x)\log 2$$

$$ \Rightarrow g'(x) = {2^x}[f'(x) + f(x)\log 2]$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$l = \int {g'(x)} dx = g(x) + C = {2^x}f(x) + C$$
